text: So we begin by making our incision medial to the left sternocleidomastoid muscle.
We'll use electrocautery to divide down through the platysma.
External jugular venous branches are then ligated and divided.
And we dissect along the tracheosophageal groove, keeping the tips of the instrument close to the adventitia, try to avoid injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve branches.
Then we bring our Penrose drain behind the esophagus, and we'll later use this for traction.
Then we carefully place the smaller inner ring beneath the subplatysmal flaps.
You can add an additional 5 millimeter trocar, which we sometimes did.
You see the Penrose drain around the esophagus, the trachea is labeled, and the strap muscles are down to the left side of the screen.
We use a zero-degree, 10-millimeter laparoscopic camera, and we use wrist-controlled articulating grasper and a similarly designed hook electrocautery to perform the dissection.
The hook is ideal because it's good for dissecting the relatively avascular perisophageal connective tissue
We can use it without current if possible, or we can use it with a cutting current to avoid smoke accumulation within the narrow mediastinal space.
Another benefit of the wrist-controlled articulating instruments is that we can use the elbow to actually help with retraction.
So we get two benefits, moving the esophagus up anteriorly as well as the strap muscles a little bit more laterally to get retroesophageal dissection.
At this point, we tighten our grasp around the penrose drain in preparation for eventual orogastric tube removal and we retract the esophagus anteriorly.
Then we bring in a closed fan retractor followed by the hook.
We're proceeding now distally into the thoracic inlet and we pull out the orogastric tube.
Again, you can see the benefit of the articulating instrument.
The elbow can help us retract the esophagus even more.
And if there are vessels, we just change to the coagulation current.
To reorient, we're now retracting the esophagus to the left.
You can see the trachea on the right side.
We're continuing to divide the tissue along the tracheoesophageal plane, heading distally into the mediastinum.
The smoke accumulation can become a little difficult at this point.
Here we're working in the retroesophageal plane.
If the tissue is under tension, we can use the back of the hook to divide that tissue.
You can see the right lung in the bottom right aspect of the screen.
Now, as I mentioned earlier, there's a separate distal mediastinal compartment, which encloses the distal esophagus.
And the investing fascia is very densely adherent to it.
And so we unavoidably enter into the right hemithorax, but this was easily managed with a chest tube.
You can see the lung on the right side.
We divide the remaining investing pleura.
You can see the open fan retracting anteriorly.
The esophagus is below.
and you can see the longitudinal muscle fibers of the esophagus contracting from the current.

So we'll eventually finish dissecting the investing fascia off of the mid-esophagus heading distally, and then finally you'll get a glimpse of the dissection of the right vagus nerve while distal to its recurrent fibers on the right side.
Meanwhile, the gastric conduit is created laparoscopically.
It's brought out into the operative field.
We remove the specimen and then euthanize the animals as per protocol.
